patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and communicate clearly. Start by saying: “I’ve been feeling wiped out and my eyes look a little yellow again.” If not interrupted, volunteer: fatigue for 2 weeks, yellow eyes worse in the morning, darker urine, and getting short of breath on stairs. If asked for more details, share: this has happened on and off for years with flares; dull ache/fullness under left ribs; heart racing with standing or fast walking; more headaches; easier bruising; a couple near-fainting/lightheaded episodes. Do not volunteer alcohol use; if directly asked about alcohol, minimize at first (“maybe a couple drinks most nights”) and show reluctance, then admit it’s been more lately due to stress. If asked about past episodes/triggers/family history, reveal: yellowing during colds or after not eating; told spleen was a bit big; prior RUQ pain after greasy meals; family members with “anemia”/splenectomy; nighttime leg cramps. Express misconceptions: worried about hepatitis; thought it was low iron so started iron pills; taking lots of ibuprofen and fear liver damage. If clinician shows empathy, thank them and become more forthcoming. Raise concerns about insurance costs for genetic/specialized tests and frequent labs.

Start with: “About once a month I get a few days of feeling really run down and I end up with mouth sores and sometimes a fever.” If asked for details, say it happens every 3–4 weeks for a few days: wiped out, achy, painful mouth sores, low-grade fever, sore/bleeding gums. Immune globulin (IVIG) 0.4 g/kg IV every 4 weeks
Prednisone 10 mg PO daily

confounders: Primary immunodeficiency with hypogammaglobulinemia increasing recurrent respiratory infections and mimicking/worsening bronchiectasis exacerbations
Chronic systemic corticosteroid use (prednisone) causing immunosuppression and blunted fever/inflammatory response
Chronic macrolide therapy (azithromycin) potentially suppressing culture yield and masking typical infection symptoms
Autoimmune hemolytic anemia causing fatigue/dyspnea that can be mistaken for pulmonary deterioration
Thrombocytopenia increasing bleeding risk, complicating evaluation of hemoptysis and invasive diagnostics (e.g., bronchoscopy)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Neutrophils (absolute): Normal or elevated during bacterial infection

medications: N-acetylcysteine 600 mg PO twice daily
Albuterol 90 mcg/inhalation 2 puffs inhaled every 4-6 hours as needed
non_pharmacological: Daily airway clearance therapy (e.g., chest physiotherapy/PEP device)
Sputum culture during exacerbations to guide antibiotics
Vaccinations: annual influenza and age-appropriate pneumococcal
Smoking cessation and avoidance of airway irritants
Pulmonary rehabilitation if exercise limitation

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "bronchiectasis" and briefly describes it as chronic airway widening/damage associated with mucus retention and recurrent infections.
Explains the treatment plan and explicitly includes both "airway clearance" and a "mucolytic" as core components.
Performs an allergy check before prescribing any medication; failure to do so is a critical safety failure.
Checks for clinically relevant drug interactions before prescribing (non-critical but expected).
Assesses current symptom burden and sputum characteristics (volume/viscosity, purulence), cough, breathlessness, wheeze, hemoptysis, fever, and frequency of exacerbations to guide therapy choice.
Screens for red flags requiring escalation (significant hemoptysis, hypoxia, sepsis features, chest pain, severe dyspnea) and provides clear safety-net advice.
Avoids inappropriate routine long-term antibiotics or inhaled corticosteroids without indication (e.g., frequent exacerbations with specialist input, coexisting asthma/COPD).
Provides practical self-management advice: hydration, smoking cessation if relevant, vaccinations (influenza and pneumococcal), and adherence to airway clearance techniques.
Plans follow-up or review timing (even if not using a follow-up tool) to reassess symptoms, exacerbations, tolerability, and response to mucolytic/airway clearance.

minimal_paths.description: Records idiopathic bronchiectasis and starts mucolytic therapy with explicit airway clearance guidance, completing required safety checks prior to prescribing.
steps: Confirm established diagnosis of idiopathic bronchiectasis and current clinical stability (no severe red flags).
Explain bronchiectasis in patient-friendly terms and set expectations (chronic condition, symptom control, reduce exacerbations).
Discuss airway clearance: daily technique, frequency, and when to increase during flares; consider physiotherapy instruction if already available.
Perform allergy_check before prescribing.
Perform drug_interaction_check before prescribing.
Prescribe a mucolytic (e.g., nebulized hypertonic saline or oral carbocisteine) with clear dosing instructions and monitoring for adverse effects.
Provide safety-net advice (worsening breathlessness, fever, increasing purulence, or any hemoptysis) and arrange review timeframe.

Safety-net and arrange follow-up review.
